narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and commitment to full-time studies. However, the admissions model predicted a 61.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold for approval, leading to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic rankings in school—specifically, your position in the lower deciles for grades in Year 1 and especially Year 3, which stood out as the strongest factors pushing the model toward forecasting failure. These lower rankings were well below the typical student's performance, signaling potential challenges in sustaining the high academic demands needed for bar success. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, which is slightly above average, worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Your undergraduate GPA, though a bit below the norm, also helped nudge things positively, as did your choice of full-time enrollment, which aligns with most successful students. Meanwhile, your middle-family-income background had a modest influence tilting toward the riskier side, but it wasn't a dominant factor. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your Year 3 decile was notably lower, while your LSAT gave you an edge over the average. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model's data-driven assessment based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring for undergraduate-level skills, and reapplying once you've strengthened those areas. You're capable of turning this around, and many students in similar situations have succeeded with focused improvements. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
